items.title: How to Build Secure AI Coding Agents with Cerebras and Docker Compose
items.author: Oleg Selajev
items.content:
In this post, we’ll dive deeper into the underlying technologies and show how the pieces come together to build an AI agent environment that’s portable, secure, and fully containerized. You’ll learn how to create multi-agent systems, run some agents with local models in Docker Model Runner, and integrate custom tools as MCP servers into your AI agent’s workflow.

We’ll also touch on how to build a secure sandbox for executing the code your agent writes, an ideal use case for containers in real-world development. 

Get the code for the agent, and prepare the .env file to provide your Cerebras API key: 

git clone https://github.com/dockersamples/docker-cerebras-demo && cd docker-cerebras-demo

# This copies the sample environment file to your local .env file
cp .env-sample .env

Now, open the file in your favorite editor and add your API key to the line. Once that’s done, run the system using Docker Compose:

docker compose up --build

The first run may take a few minutes to pull the model and containers. Once it’s up, you can see the agent at .

The first run may take a few minutes to pull the necessary Docker images and the AI model. Once it’s running, you can access the agent’s interface at . From there, you can interact with your agent and issue commands like “write code,” “initialize the sandbox environment,” or request specific tools like “cerebras, curl docker.com for me please.”

Understanding the Architecture

  1. The Agentic Loop: This is the main application logic that orchestrates the agent’s behavior. In our case, it’s an ADK-Python-based application. The ADK-Python framework also includes a visualizer that lets you inspect tool calls and trace how the system reached specific decisions.
  1. The MCP Tools: These are the external tools the agent can use. We provide them securely via the Docker MCP Gateway. In this app we use context7 and node sandbox MCP servers. 
  2. The AI Model: You can define any local or remote AI model you want to use. Here, we’re using a local Qwen model for routing between the local agent and the powerful Cerebras agent which will use Cerebras API. 

Cerebras Cloud serves as a specialized, high-performance inference backend. It can run massive models, like a half-trillion parameter Qwen coder, at thousands of tokens per second. While our simple demo doesn’t require this level of speed, such performance is a game-changer for real-world applications.

Most of the prompts and responses are a few hundred tokens long, as they are simple commands to initialize a sandbox or write some JavaScript code in it. You’re welcome to make the agent work harder and see Cerebras’ performance on more verbose requests. 

For example, you can ask the Cerebras agent to write some JavaScript code, and see it call the functions from the MCP tools to read and write the files and run them as you see on the screenshot below. 

Building a Custom Sandbox as an MCP Server

A key feature of this setup is the ability to create a secure sandbox for code execution. To do this, we’ll build a custom MCP server. In our example, we enable two MCP servers:

  • : This gives our agent access to the latest documentation for various application frameworks.
  • : This is our custom-made sandbox for executing the code our agent writes.

You can find the implementation of our Node.js sandbox server in the node-sandbox-mcp GitHub repository. It’s a Quarkus application written in Java that exposes itself as an mcp-server and uses the awesome Testcontainers library to create and manage the sandbox containers programmatically.

An important detail is that you have full control over the sandbox configuration. We start the container with a common Node.js development image and, as a crucial security measure, disable its networking. But since it’s a custom MCP server, you can enable any security measures you deem necessary. 

Here’s a snippet of the Testcontainers-java code used to create the container:

GenericContainer sandboxContainer = new GenericContainer<>("mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/javascript-node:20")
                .withNetworkMode("none") // disable network!!
                .withWorkingDirectory("/workspace")
                .withCommand("sleep", "infinity");

 sandboxContainer.start();

Testcontainers provides a flexible, idiomatic API to interact with the sandbox. Running a command or writing a file becomes a simple one-line method call:

// To execute a command inside the sandbox
sandbox.execInContainer(command);

// To write a file into the sandbox
sandbox.copyFileToContainer(Transferable.of(contents.getBytes()), filename);

The actual implementation has a bit more glue code for managing background processes or selecting the correct sandbox if you’ve created multiple, but these one-liners are the core of the interaction.

Packaging and Using the Custom Server

To use our custom server, we first need to package it as a Docker image. For Quarkus applications, a single command does the trick:

./mvnw package -DskipTests=true -Dquarkus.container-image.build=true

This command produces a local Docker image and outputs its name, something like:

[INFO] [io.quarkus.container.image.docker.deployment.DockerProcessor] Built container image shelajev/node-sandbox:1.0.0-SNAPSHOT

Since we’re running everything locally, we don’t even need to push this image to a remote registry. You can inspect this image in Docker Desktop and find its hash, which we’ll use in the next step.

Integrating the Sandbox via the MCP Gateway

With our custom MCP server image ready, it’s time to plug it into the MCP Gateway. We’ll create a custom catalog file (mcp-gateway-catalog.yaml) that enables both the standard server and our new .

Currently, creating this file is a manual process, but we’re working on simplifying it. The result is a portable catalog file that mixes standard and custom MCP servers.

Notice two key things in the configuration for the node-code-sandbox MCP server in the catalog:

  • : This tells the gateway that our server needs to persist between the tool calls to track the sandbox’s state. 
  • : We reference the specific Docker image using its hash to ensure reproducibility.

If you’re building the custom server for the sandbox MCP, you can replace the image reference with the one your build step produced. 

    longLived: true
    image: olegselajev241/node-sandbox@sha256:44437d5b61b6f324d3bb10c222ac43df9a5b52df9b66d97a89f6e0f8d8899f67

Finally, we update our to mount this catalog file and enable both servers:

  mcp-gateway:
    # mcp-gateway secures your MCP servers
    image: docker/mcp-gateway:latest
    use_api_socket: true
    command:
      - --transport=sse
      # add any MCP servers you want to use
      - --servers=context7,node-code-sandbox
      - --catalog=/mcp-gateway-catalog.yaml
    volumes:
      - ./mcp-gateway-catalog.yaml:/mcp-gateway-catalog.yaml:ro

When you run , the gateway starts, which in turn starts our MCP server. When the agent requests a sandbox, a third container is launched – the actual isolated environment. 

You can use tools like Docker Desktop to inspect all running containers, view files, or even open a shell for debugging.

The Security Benefits of Containerized Sandboxes 

This containerized sandbox approach is a significant security win. Containers provide a well-understood security boundary with a smaller vulnerability profile than running random internet code on your host machine, and you can harden them as needed.

Remember how we disabled networking in the sandbox container? This means any code the agent generates cannot leak local secrets or data to the internet. If you ask the agent to run code that tries to access, for example, , it will fail.

This demonstrates a key advantage: granular control. While the sandbox is cut off from the network, other tools are not. The MCP server can still access the internet to fetch documentation, allowing the agent to write better code without compromising the security of the execution environment.

Oh, and a neat detail is that when you stop the containers managed by compose, it also kills the sandbox MCP server, and that in turn triggers Testcontainers to clean up all the sandbox containers, just like it cleans after a typical test run. 

Next Steps and Extensibility

This coding agent is a great starting point, but it isn’t production-ready. For a real-world application, you might want to grant controlled access to resources like the npm registry. You could, for example, achieve this by mapping your local npm cache from the host system into the sandbox. This way, you, the developer, control exactly which npm libraries are accessible.

Because the sandbox is a custom MCP server, the possibilities are endless. You can build it yourself, tweak it however you want, and integrate any tools or constraints you need.

In this post, we demonstrated how to build a secure and portable AI coding agent using Docker Compose and the MCP Toolkit. By creating a custom MCP server with Testcontainers, we built a sandboxed execution environment that offers granular security controls, like disabling network access, without limiting the agent’s other tools.  We connect this coding agent to Cerebras API, so we get incredible inference speed. This architecture provides a powerful and secure foundation for building your own AI agents. We encourage you to clone the repository and experiment with the code! You probably already have Docker and can sign up for a Cerebras API key here.

items.title: Kubernetes v1.34: Moving Volume Group Snapshots to v1beta2
items.content:
Volume group snapshots were introduced as an Alpha feature with the Kubernetes 1.27 release and moved to Beta in the Kubernetes 1.32 release. The recent release of Kubernetes v1.34 moved that support to a second beta. The support for volume group snapshots relies on a set of extension APIs for group snapshots. These APIs allow users to take crash consistent snapshots for a set of volumes. Behind the scenes, Kubernetes uses a label selector to group multiple PersistentVolumeClaims for snapshotting. A key aim is to allow you restore that set of snapshots to new volumes and recover your workload based on a crash consistent recovery point.

This new feature is only supported for CSI volume drivers.

While testing the beta version, we encountered an issue where the field is not set for individual VolumeSnapshotContents and VolumeSnapshots if CSI driver does not implement the ListSnapshots RPC call. We evaluated various options here and decided to make this change releasing a new beta for the API.

Specifically, a VolumeSnapshotInfo struct is added in v1beta2, it contains information for an individual volume snapshot that is a member of a volume group snapshot. VolumeSnapshotInfoList, a list of VolumeSnapshotInfo, is added to VolumeGroupSnapshotContentStatus, replacing VolumeSnapshotHandlePairList. VolumeSnapshotInfoList is a list of snapshot information returned by the CSI driver to identify snapshots on the storage system. VolumeSnapshotInfoList is populated by the csi-snapshotter sidecar based on the CSI CreateVolumeGroupSnapshotResponse returned by the CSI driver's CreateVolumeGroupSnapshot call.

The existing v1beta1 API objects will be converted to the new v1beta2 API objects by a conversion webhook.

Depending on feedback and adoption, the Kubernetes project plans to push the volume group snapshot implementation to general availability (GA) in a future release.

This project, like all of Kubernetes, is the result of hard work by many contributors from diverse backgrounds working together. On behalf of SIG Storage, I would like to offer a huge thank you to the contributors who stepped up these last few quarters to help the project reach beta:

For those interested in getting involved with the design and development of CSI or any part of the Kubernetes Storage system, join the Kubernetes Storage Special Interest Group (SIG). We always welcome new contributors.

items.title: MCP Security: A Developer’s Guide
items.author: Saurabh Davala
items.content:
Since its release by Anthropic in November 2024, Model Context Protocol (MCP) has gained massive adoption and is quickly becoming the connective tissue between AI agents and the tools, APIs, and data they act on. 

With just a few lines of configuration, an agent can search code, open tickets, query SaaS systems, or even deploy infrastructure. That kind of flexibility is powerful but it also introduces new security challenges. In fact, security researchers analyzing the MCP ecosystem found command injection flaws affecting 43% of analyzed servers. A single misconfigured or malicious server can exfiltrate secrets, trigger unsafe actions, or quietly change how an agent behaves. 

This guide is for developers and platform teams building with agents. We’ll unpack what makes MCP workflows uniquely risky for AI infrastructure, highlight common missteps like prompt injection or shadow tooling, and show how secure defaults, like containerized MCP servers and policy-based gateways, can help you govern every tool call without slowing your AI roadmap.

Model Context Protocol is a standardized interface that enables AI agents to interact with external tools, databases, and services. MCP security refers to the controls and risks that govern how agents discover, connect to, and execute MCP servers. These security risks span across the entire development lifecycle and involve:

  • : how servers are packaged, signed, versioned, and approved.
  • : how they’re executed on the host vs. in containers, with CPU/memory/network limits.
  • : how calls are mediated, logged, blocked, or transformed in real time.
  • : which tools a given IDE/agent is allowed to see and use.

Why does MCP security matter?

Securing MCP workflows has become more important than ever because AI agents blur the line between “code” and “runtime.” A prompt or tool description can change what your system is capable of without a code release. 

This means that security practices have to move up a layer, from static analysis to policy over agent‑tool interactions. Docker codifies that policy in a gateway and makes secure defaults practical for everyday developers.

Docker’s approach is to make MCP both and through containerized execution, a policy‑enforcing , and a curated that helps teams standardize what agents can do. If you’re building with agents, this guide will help you understand the risks, why traditional tools fall short, and how Docker reduces blast radius without slowing your AI roadmap.

Understanding MCP security risks

While MCP risks can show up in various ways across the dev lifecycle, there are specific categories they typically fall into. The section below highlights how these risks surface in real workflows, their impact, and practical guardrails that mitigate without slowing teams down. 

Misconfigurations & weak defaults

  • Running servers directly on the host with broad privileges or a persistent state.
  • Unrestricted network egress from tools to the public internet.
  • Unvetted catalogs/registries in client configs, exposing agents to unknown tools.
  • for tool calls-hard to investigate or respond.

Lateral movement, data exfiltration, and irreproducible behavior.

Always follow MCP server best practices such as leveraging containerization, applying resource and network limits, maintaining an allowlist of approved tools, and capturing call logs centrally.

Malicious or compromised servers (supply chain)

  • Typosquatting/poisoned images or unsigned builds.
  • or altered tool metadata that nudges agents into risky actions.

Covert behavior change, credential theft, persistent access.

Require signature verification, pin versions/digests, and pull from curated sources such as the .

Secret management failures

  • in environment variables, prompts, or tool arguments.
  • via tool outputs or model completions.

Account takeover, data loss.

Use managed secrets, minimize prompt exposure, and redact or block sensitive values at the broker.

Prompt injection & tool poisoning

  • : hostile content instructs the model to exfiltrate data or call dangerous tools.
  • : misleading tool descriptions or unexpected defaults that steer the agent.

Agents do the wrong thing, confidently.

Strict tool allowlists, pre/post‑call interceptors, and output filtering at the gateway. Docker’s MCP Gateway provides active security capabilities (signature checks, call logging, secret and network controls, interceptors).

What makes MCP security challenging?

  • Dynamic & non‑deterministic behavior: the same prompt may lead to different tool calls.
  • Instruction vs. data ambiguity: LLMs can treat content (including tool docs) as instructions.
  • Growing, shifting attack surface: every new tool expands what the agent can do instantly.
  • : Static analysis tools don’t see agentic tool calls or MCP semantics; you need mediation between agents and tools, not just better prompts.

Implication for developers: You need a guardrail that lives at the agent–tool boundary, verifying what runs, brokering what’s allowed, and recording what happened.

How to prevent and mitigate MCP server security concerns

Use this practitioner checklist to raise the floor:

  1. Containerize every MCP serverRun servers in containers (not on the host) with and a read‑only filesystem where possible. Treat each server as untrusted code with the least privilege necessary.Why it helps limits blast radius and makes behavior reproducible.
  2. Centralize enforcement at a gateway (broker)Place a between clients (IDE/agent) and servers. Use it to:
    • Verify before running servers.
    • Maintain a (only approved servers are discoverable).
    • Apply and .
    • requests/responses for audit and incident response.
  3. Govern secrets end‑to‑endStore secrets in a managed system; avoid .env files. Prefer short‑lived tokens. Sanitize prompts and tool outputs to reduce exposure.
  4. Use (argument/type checks, safety classifiers) and (redaction, PII scrub). Combine with strict tool scoping to reduce prompt‑injection blast radius.
  5. Pull servers from curated sources (e.g., ), require signatures, and pin to immutable versions.
  6. Alert on anomalous tool sequences (e.g., sudden credential access), and run tabletop exercises to rotate tokens and revoke access.

How Docker makes MCP security practical

Turning MCP security from theory into practice means putting guardrails where agents meet tools and making trusted servers easy to adopt for agentic workflows. Docker’s MCP stack does both: Docker Gateway enforces policy and observability on every call, while the Docker MCP Catalog & Toolkit curates, verifies, and versions the servers your team can safely use.

Docker MCP Gateway: Your enforcement point

The gateway sits between clients and servers to provide verification, policy, and observability for every tool call. It supports active security measures like signature verification, call logging, secret and network controls, and pre/post-interceptors so you can block or transform risky actions before they reach your systems. 

Docker MCP Catalog & Toolkit: Curation and convenience

Use the to standardize the servers your organization trusts. The catalog helps reduce supply‑chain risk (publisher verification, versioning, provenance) and makes it straightforward for developers to pull approved tools into their workflow. With a growing selection of 150+ curated MCP servers, MCP Catalog is a safe and easy way to get started with MCP.

Looking for a broader view of how Docker helps with AI development? Check out .

Putting it all Together: A practical flow

  1. Choose servers from the Catalog and pin them by digest.
  2. Register servers with the Gateway so clients only see approved tooling.
  3. : verify signatures, log all calls, redact/deny secrets, and restrict egress.
  4. Add pre/post interceptors: validate arguments (before), redact/normalize outputs (after).
  5. : review call logs, alert on anomalies, rotate secrets, and update allowlists as new tools are introduced.

MCP unlocks powerful agentic workflows but also introduces new classes of risk, from prompt injection to tool poisoning and supply‑chain tampering. MCP security isn’t just better prompts; it’s secure packaging, verified distribution, and a brokered runtime with policy.

Browse the: Discover 200+ containerized, security-hardened MCP servers

Download the MCP Toolkit in: Get immediate access to secure credential management and container isolation

items.title: Kubernetes v1.34: Decoupled Taint Manager Is Now Stable
items.content:
This enhancement separates the responsibility of managing node lifecycle and pod eviction into two distinct components. Previously, the node lifecycle controller handled both marking nodes as unhealthy with NoExecute taints and evicting pods from them. Now, a dedicated taint eviction controller manages the eviction process, while the node lifecycle controller focuses solely on applying taints. This separation not only improves code organization but also makes it easier to improve taint eviction controller or build custom implementations of the taint based eviction.

The feature gate SeparateTaintEvictionController has been promoted to GA in this release. Users can optionally disable taint-based eviction by setting --controllers=-taint-eviction-controller in kube-controller-manager.

We offer a huge thank you to all the contributors who helped with design, implementation, and review of this feature and helped move it from beta to stable:

items.title: AWS named as a Leader in 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms and Container Management
items.author: Channy Yun (윤석찬)
items.content:
Today, I’m happy to share recent Magic Quadrant reports that named AWS as a Leader in more cloud technology markets: Cloud-Native Application Platforms (aka Cloud Application Platforms) and Container Management.

2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms AWS has been named a Leader in the Gartner Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms for 2 consecutive years. AWS was positioned highest on “Ability to Execute”. Gartner defines cloud-native application platforms as those that provide managed application runtime environments for applications and integrated capabilities to manage the lifecycle of an application or application component in the cloud environment.

The following image is the graphical representation of the 2025 Magic Quadrant for Cloud-Native Application Platforms.

Our comprehensive cloud-native application portfolio—AWS Lambda, AWS App Runner, AWS Amplify, and AWS Elastic Beanstalk—offers flexible options for building modern applications with strong AI capabilities, demonstrated through continued innovation and deep integration across our broader AWS service portfolio.

You can simplify the service selection through comprehensive documentation, reference architectures, and prescriptive guidance available in the AWS Solutions Library, along with AI-powered, contextual recommendations from Amazon Q based on your specific requirements. While AWS Lambda is optimized for AWS to provide the best possible serverless experience, it follows industry standards for serverless computing and supports common programming languages and frameworks. You can find all necessary capabilities within AWS, including advanced features for AI/ML, edge computing, and enterprise integration.

2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Container Management In the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for Container Management, AWS has been named as a Leader for three years and was positioned furthest for “Completeness of Vision”. Gartner defines container management as offerings that support the deployment and operation of containerized workloads. This process involves orchestrating and overseeing the entire lifecycle of containers, covering deployment, scaling, and operations, to ensure their efficient and consistent performance across different environments.

The following image is the graphical representation of the 2025 Magic Quadrant for Container Management.

AWS container services offer fully managed container orchestration with AWS native solutions and open-source technologies to focus on providing a wide range of deployment options, from Kubernetes to our native orchestrator.

items.title: Rails Security in 2025: Automate or Get Breached
items.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
items.content:
When it comes to web applications, speed and features matter, but security is non-negotiable. A single breach can cost more than downtime — it can damage brand trust, trigger compliance penalties, and erode customer confidence.

Rails has long been a pioneer in , shipping with built-in protections like CSRF tokens, SQL injection guards, and strong parameter enforcement. But in 2025, with evolving cyber threats, secure defaults aren’t enough — you need active, automated defenses.

🔍 Why Rails Security Still Matters in 2025

  • Attackers increasingly use to exploit weak spots faster.
  • Supply chain vulnerabilities (gem dependencies) remain a hidden risk.
  • amplify the damage of small mistakes.

Even a simple missed parameter sanitization can open the door to data leaks.

⚡ Automating Security with Rails

Manual audits are no longer practical — security has to be .

Brakeman is a static analysis tool built for Rails. It scans your codebase for vulnerabilities like SQL injection, XSS, and unsafe mass assignments.

jobs:  security_scan:    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Run Brakeman          gem install brakeman

With this, every PR automatically gets a , ensuring no risky code slips into production.

Use to scan for vulnerable gems.

bundle audit check --update

This helps you patch known CVEs before attackers exploit them.

3. Threat Modeling & Logs

  • Enable by default (config.action_dispatch.default_headers).
  • Enforce content security policies (CSP) to prevent injection.
  • Ship logs to a SIEM (Splunk, Datadog, Elastic) to detect unusual request spikes.

⚠️ Lessons from Real-World Breaches

  1. : Apps that disabled authenticity tokens for convenience later saw unauthorized fund transfers.
  • : Always enforce protect_from_forgery (enabled by default).

2. SQL Injection via User Input: A fintech app interpolated strings into queries.

  • : Always use (where("email = ?", input)).

: Failing to whitelist strong params allowed users to change their roles.

  • : Always use (params.require(:user).permit(:email, :name)).

Each of these could have been prevented by Rails defaults + automated audits.

Security isn’t a  — it’s an ongoing process. Rails makes it easier with its , but you need to go further:

  • Automate audits with .
  • Continuously monitor dependencies.
  • Review real-world breaches and ensure your app enforces strong parameters, CSPs, and CSRF protections.

If you’re running a SaaS or enterprise app on Rails, now is the time to:

  • Add to your CI/CD.
  • Run a  today.
  • Review your for potential gaps.

🔐 Security isn’t optional — . Build it before attackers test your walls.

items.title: Rails Meets Intelligence: Two Proven Patterns for Adding AI to Your App (OpenAI API + a Python ML…
items.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
items.content:
Rails Meets Intelligence: Two Proven Patterns for Adding AI to Your App (OpenAI API + a Python ML Microservice)

Want AI in your Rails app  — without rewriting your stack? This guide gives you two production-ready patterns:

  1. with OpenAI (great for chat assistants, summarization, semantic search/RAG).
  2. via a small microservice (great when you need your own models, control, or air-gapped deploys).

We’ll build both, wire them into Rails, and show where each shines. Copy-paste friendly, with tests, jobs, and Docker compose.

Notes on freshness: we use OpenAI’s (the modern, unified endpoint) and for RAG. Double-check model names and parameters in the official docs when you ship.
  • An service calling OpenAI’s .
  • using OpenAI + Postgres .
  • A that calls your microservice.
  • Background jobs (Sidekiq) for long-running work.

You can use each part independently.

  • handles UI, persistence, and orchestration.
  • : text reasoning, summarization, Q&A, and RAG answering.
  • : a small container exposing /predict, loading a scikit-learn pipeline.
  • : store embeddings & run vector search.
  • : background jobs & retries.
  • Postgres 14+ with pgvector
  • Docker (optional but recommended)

Part A — API-based AI in Rails (OpenAI Responses + Embeddings)

gem "faraday"gem "faraday_middleware"gem "sidekiq"

Postgres extension & column

# db/migrate/20240901000000_enable_pgvector.rbclass EnablePgvector < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.1]    enable_extension "vector" unless extension_enabled?("vector")end
# db/migrate/20240901000001_add_embeddings_to_docs.rb
class AddEmbeddingsToDocs < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.1]    add_column :documents, :embedding, :vector, limit: 1536 # choose dims to match your model
    add_index  :documents, :embedding, using: :hnsw, opclass: :vector_cosine_ops
  end
The pgvector gem provides native vector types and ANN indexes (hnsw, ivfflat), with cosine/inner-product/L2 ops.
# .env or credentialsOPENAI_API_KEY=...
Reminder: OpenAI docs list the /v1/embeddings). They also show modern model families like text-embedding-3-small/large

3) A tiny OpenAI client (Faraday)

# app/lib/openai_client.rbclass OpenAIClient
  def initialize(api_key: ENV.fetch("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
    @conn = Faraday.new(url: "https://api.openai.com/v1") do |f|      f.response :json, content_type: / json$/
      f.headers["Authorization"] = "Bearer #{api_key}"
      f.adapter Faraday.default_adapter  end
# Responses API (general text/chat)
  def respond(instructions:, input:, model: "gpt-4o-mini", max_output_tokens: 800)
    @conn.post("responses") do |req|
      req.body = {        instructions: instructions, # like a system prompt
        input: input,               # string or array of content parts
        max_output_tokens: max_output_tokens    end.body  # Embeddings for semantic search / RAG
  def embed(input:, model: "text-embedding-3-small", dimensions: nil)
    body = { model: model, input: input }
    body[:dimensions] = dimensions if dimensions
    @conn.post("embeddings") { |r| r.body = body }.bodyend
instructions (system-like) and input, with output text available in the response body (client SDKs often expose output_text). Embeddings support an optional dimensions arg for shortening vectors. See docs and model pages for exact fields and current models.

4) Seed a tiny knowledge base

# app/models/document.rbclass Document < ApplicationRecord
  # fields: title, body:text, embedding:vector
  validates :title, :body, presence: true
rails g model Document title:string body:textrails db:migrate

5) Background job to embed documents

# config/initializers/sidekiq.rbRails.application.config.active_job.queue_adapter = :sidekiq
# app/jobs/embed_document_job.rbclass EmbedDocumentJob < ApplicationJob    doc   = Document.find(document_id)
    text  = "#{doc.title}

#{doc.body}"
    openai = OpenAIClient.new
    vec = openai.embed(input: text)["data"][0]["embedding"]
    doc.update!(embedding: vec)end
Sidekiq is a high-performance background job framework for Ruby and integrates easily with Rails. Use it for long calls, retries, and scheduling.

When you create or update a document:

# app/models/document.rbafter_commit -> { EmbedDocumentJob.perform_later(id) }, on: [:create, :update]

6) Vector search (pgvector) + RAG answerer

# app/services/semantic_search.rbclass SemanticSearch
  def initialize(k: 5); @k = k; end
    openai = OpenAIClient.new
    qvec = openai.embed(input: query)["data"][0]["embedding"]
    # cosine distance operator: <-> with vector_cosine_ops index      .select("documents.*, (embedding <-> ARRAY[#{qvec.join(',')}]) AS distance")      .limit(@k)end
# app/services/rag_answerer.rbclass RagAnswerer    @openai = OpenAIClient.new    top_docs = SemanticSearch.new.nearest(question)
    context  = top_docs.map { |d| "#{d.title}:
#{d.body}" }.join("
---
")
    resp = @openai.respond(
      instructions: "You are a helpful, concise Rails expert. Cite facts only from the provided context.",
      input: [        "QUESTION:
#{question}",
        "Answer with bullet points and include a short sources list using document titles."
      ],      max_output_tokens: 500    # Depending on SDK/response shape; extract text robustly:  end  def extract_text(resp)
    # Common pattern: resp["output"] -> array of content parts -> text
    text = resp.dig("output", 0, "content", 0, "text")  end
text-embedding-3-small is a cost-efficient default for search/RAG; you can shorten embedding dimensions if needed.
# config/routes.rbresource :assistant, only: [:create]
# app/controllers/assistants_controller.rbclass AssistantsController < ApplicationController    question = params.require(:question)
    answer   = RagAnswerer.new.call(question: question)
    render json: { answer: answer }end

OpenAI states that, , data sent via the API isn’t used to train their models. Confirm your org’s settings and agreements.

Part B — A Python ML Microservice (FastAPI + scikit-learn)

This gives you : your own models, offline capability, custom features, and separate scaling.

# ml_service/app.pyfrom fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
import joblibmodel = Noneclass PredictIn(BaseModel):class PredictOut(BaseModel):    score: float@app.on_event("startup")    global model        if model is None:
            model = joblib.load("model.pkl")
@app.post("/predict", response_model=PredictOut)
def predict(payload: PredictIn):
    proba = model.predict_proba([payload.text])[0]    label = model.classes_[idx]
    score = float(proba[idx])
    return {"label": label, "score": score, "model_version": "1.0.0"}

Install & run FastAPI (dev):

pip install "fastapi[standard]" scikit-learn joblibfastapi dev ml_service/app.py  # auto-reload
The FastAPI docs show the modern fastapi[standard] extra for a batteries-included dev server and docs at 

2) Train a tiny model (toy sentiment)

# ml_service/train.pyfrom sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression  "I love this product", "amazing build quality", "works great",
  "this is awful", "I hate it", "terrible support"
]
y = ["pos", "pos", "pos", "neg", "neg", "neg"]
pipe = Pipeline([
  ("vec", TfidfVectorizer(ngram_range=(1,2), min_df=1)),
  ("clf", LogisticRegression(max_iter=500))pipe.fit(X, y)
joblib.dump(pipe, "model.pkl")
print("Saved model.pkl"
python ml_service/train.py

3) Dockerize the microservice

# ml_service/DockerfileFROM python:3.11-slimCOPY ml_service/requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY ml_service/ .CMD ["fastapi", "run", "app.py", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
# ml_service/requirements.txtfastapi[standard]joblib
# app/services/sentiment_client.rbclass SentimentClient
  def initialize(base_url: ENV.fetch("ML_SERVICE_URL", "http://ml:8000"))
    @conn = Faraday.new(url: base_url) do |f|      f.response :json, content_type: / json$/
      f.adapter Faraday.default_adapter
    end    @conn.post("/predict") { |r| r.body = { text: text } }.bodyend

5) Background job + circuit-friendly retries

# app/jobs/sentiment_job.rbclass SentimentJob < ApplicationJob
rescue_from(StandardError) do |e|
    retry_job wait: 5.seconds, queue: :low  def perform(review_id)
    review = Review.find(review_id)
    res = SentimentClient.new.predict(review.body)
    review.update!(sentiment_label: res["label"], sentiment_score: res["score"])
  end

Docker Compose (Rails + ML + Postgres + Redis)

# docker-compose.ymlservices:    image: postgres:15      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres    volumes: ["pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data"]    image: redis:7    build: ./ml_service  web:    command: bash -lc "bundle exec rails s -b 0.0.0.0"      DATABASE_URL: postgres://postgres:postgres@db:5432/postgres
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${OPENAI_API_KEY}
      ML_SERVICE_URL: http://ml:8000
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    depends_on: ["db", "redis", "ml"]
  worker:    command: bash -lc "bundle exec sidekiq"      DATABASE_URL: postgres://postgres:postgres@db:5432/postgres
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${OPENAI_API_KEY}
      ML_SERVICE_URL: http://ml:8000
    depends_on: ["db", "redis", "ml"]  pgdata:
# spec/services/openai_client_spec.rbrequire "rails_helper"
RSpec.describe OpenAIClient do
  it "embeds text" do
    stub = stub_request(:post, "https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings")
      .to_return(body: { data: [ { embedding: [0.1, 0.2] } ] }.to_json, headers: { "Content-Type" => "application/json" })
    out = described_class.new.embed(input: "hello")
    expect(out["data"][0]["embedding"]).to eq([0.1, 0.2])
    expect(stub).to have_been_requested
  end
# tests/test_app.pyfrom fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from app import app, load_model
    load_model()    res = client.post("/predict", json={"text": "great job"})
    assert res.status_code == 200
    data = res.json()
    assert "label" in data and "score" in data
  • : fastest path to useful features (assistants, summarization, reasoning, code help, RAG). Great for launch; you pay per use, no infra for training.
  • : when you need custom features, offline, special models, or strict governance. FastAPI gives you typed I/O, docs at /docs, and easy scaling.
  • : add embeddings + pgvector to ground model answers in your data; cosine distance + ANN indexes keep it snappy.
  • : use Rails credentials / env vars.
  • : set Faraday timeouts; use Sidekiq retries.
  • : log prompt/latency (with redaction), tag job IDs.
  • : cache embeddings and responses when inputs repeat.
  • : rate-limit endpoints; auth your FastAPI service (e.g., shared token header).
  • : confirm vendor data handling for your org.

Step-by-step: add this to an existing Rails app (quick path)

  1. (faraday, pgvector, sidekiq) and run migrations enabling vector.
  2. OpenAIClient and RagAnswerer services.
  3. the Document model + embedding job.
  4. the FastAPI microservice, train the toy model, dockerize.
  5. services (db, redis, ml, web, worker) with environment variables.
  6. a controller endpoint (/assistant) and a background SentimentJob.
  7.  — set monitors, budgets, and alerts.
<!-- app/views/home/index.html.erb --><h1>Ask the knowledge base</h1>
<%= form_with url: assistant_path, method: :post, data: { turbo: false } do |f| %>
  <%= f.text_area :question, rows: 3 %><% end %>
<h2>Analyze sentiment</h2>
<%= form_with url: reviews_path, method: :post, data: { turbo: false } do |f| %>
  <%= f.text_area :body, rows: 3 %>
  <%= f.submit "Classify" %>
# app/controllers/reviews_controller.rbclass ReviewsController < ApplicationController    review = Review.create!(body: params[:body])
    SentimentJob.perform_later(review.id)
    redirect_to root_path, notice: "Queued for classification"end
  • Can I stream Responses to the UI? Yes — use server-sent events or WebSockets; the Responses API supports streaming. Check the official examples for current parameters.
  • Start with text-embedding-3-small for cost/quality, move to -large if needed; consider dimensions to shorten vectors. You can use Sidekiq directly or via Active Job; Sidekiq’s wiki covers best practices, scaling, and retries.

Clean, opinionated defaults

  • : keep prompts and extraction in service objects.
  • : always show doc titles in answers; keep the model grounded.
  • : typed inputs/outputs with Pydantic; one endpoint per task; add auth header.

Want this tailored to your SaaS?

Tell me your domain (CRM, analytics, marketplace, fintech, edtech…) and I’ll adapt:

  • Entities (e.g., , )
  • Prompts + retrieval schema
  • Docker compose + deploy notes (Fly/Render/K8s)

You’ll get ready-to-paste partials and tests wired to your data model.

By combining with  — whether through APIs like OpenAI or custom microservices built with FastAPI — you unlock a powerful stack that’s both developer-friendly and production-ready.

  • Rails stays lean, handling user flows, jobs, and UI.
  • Python takes care of heavy ML lifting (training, inference, embeddings).
  • Tools like and make orchestration and retrieval fast at scale.

👉 The result? SaaS apps that feel smarter, deliver personalized experiences, and stand out in a crowded market — without rewriting everything in a single tech stack.

If you’re building a CRM, marketplace, or analytics SaaS and want to see how AI could be plugged directly into your domain (e.g., smart deal recommendations, predictive pricing, or AI-driven dashboards), let me know — I’ll publish a tailored version with copy-paste partials, jobs, and tests.

✨ : Try setting up a small proof-of-concept today — wire Rails to a simple FastAPI service, call OpenAI, and log results in your database. From there, you can grow into full semantic search, recommendation engines, or AI copilots.

items.title: From REST to GraphQL: Supercharging Rails APIs in 2025
items.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
items.content:
In today’s world of and , APIs are the backbone of every user experience. Traditionally, Rails developers leaned on  — simple, reliable, and widely adopted. But REST has limitations: over-fetching, under-fetching, and rigid endpoints that don’t scale well across multiple frontends.

That’s where GraphQL with Rails (via graphql-ruby) comes in. Instead of hitting multiple REST endpoints, clients can query exactly the data they need — nothing more, nothing less.

🔍 Why Move from REST to GraphQL?

  • : One query instead of multiple REST calls.
  • : Mobile, web, and even IoT devices can shape responses differently.
  • : Less payload → faster apps.
  • : A single endpoint that serves many frontend variations.

⚡ Implementing GraphQL in a Rails App

# Gemfilegem "graphql"
gem "graphiql-rails", group: :development
bundle installrails generate graphql:install

This sets up /graphql as your endpoint and enables in development for interactive queries.

2. Define Your First Schema

Rails generates a Types folder. Let’s define a simple :

# app/graphql/types/post_type.rbmodule Types
  class PostType < Types::BaseObject
    field :id, ID, null: false
    field :title, String, null: false
    field :content, String, null: true
    field :author, String, null: false
  end
# app/graphql/types/query_type.rbmodule Types
  class QueryType < Types::BaseObject
    field :posts, [Types::PostType], null: false      Post.all  end

And get a shaped to your needs.

📈 Measuring Performance Gains

  • : To fetch posts and authors, you may hit /posts + /authors.
  • : A single query returns posts .

Result: Fewer network calls, smaller payloads, faster render times.

🎯 Best Practices for GraphQL in Rails

  • Keep queries  — avoid nested queries that become expensive.
  • Use (connections) to handle large data sets.
  • Implement at the field level.
  • Use caching (Apollo, Dataloader) for performance.

GraphQL isn’t just hype — it’s becoming the standard for . Rails developers now have the tools to:

  • Serve multiple clients (web, mobile, admin dashboards) from one endpoint.
  • Deliver faster, more flexible user experiences.

If your Rails app still runs on , try setting up a this week. Start with one resource (like posts) and measure the difference in API calls, payload size, and response times.

🚀 The shift from REST to GraphQL could be the upgrade your SaaS backend needs in 2025.

items.title: Performance & Scalability Enhancements in Rails 8: Faster Apps, Lower Costs
items.author: Vaishnavi Ganeshkar
items.content:
When you’re building a SaaS product, performance isn’t just a developer concern — it’s a business one. Faster response times mean happier users, better retention, and lower cloud bills. With Rails 8, the framework continues to sharpen its edge, focusing on memory efficiency, concurrency, and caching improvements that directly translate to ROI.

Let’s break down what’s new, why it matters, and how you can put it into practice.

🚀 Rails 8 Performance Upgrades

1. ActiveRecord Efficiency

Rails 8 introduces smarter query generation and reduced object allocations. By minimizing intermediate objects and cutting down redundant SQL, heavy queries now execute faster with less memory overhead.

# Old behaviorUser.where(active: true).select(:id).map(&:id)
# New optimized behaviorUser.where(active: true).pluck(:id)

This change looks small but can save tens of milliseconds per request when dealing with thousands of records.

Rails 8 expands support for (Ruby’s concurrency model) and leverages YJIT improvements in Ruby 3.3, allowing apps to serve more requests per second without extra servers.

Result: You can scale (buy fewer servers initially) while handling more throughput.

Caching isn’t new, but Rails 8 makes Russian doll caching and fragment caching more efficient by:

  • Reducing cache key collisions.
  • Handling nested template rendering faster.
  • Supporting modern Redis/Memcached backends seamlessly.

This matters because the right cache strategy can cut 40–60% of redundant DB hits, directly reducing your cloud costs.

4. Parallel Testing Improvements

Tests now run faster by default with parallelization improvements in Rails 8. CI pipelines that used to take 15 minutes can now finish in 6–8.

This means your dev team ships features quicker while maintaining quality.

📊 Business ROI: Why This Matters for Enterprises

  • : Efficient queries and better caching reduce DB load, saving thousands in AWS/GCP bills.
  • : Every 100ms shaved off page loads improves engagement and conversion rates.
  • : Improved test parallelization speeds up feedback loops, so features ship faster.

One Rails SaaS team saw a 25% drop in Postgres costs just by moving from raw .map queries to pluck and introducing Russian doll caching on their dashboards.

Rails has always been about , but Rails 8 shows how it’s also about . With faster queries, better concurrency, and smarter caching, you don’t just get snappier apps — you get measurable ROI in reduced infrastructure spend and faster delivery cycles.

items.title: Using Maven Wrapper to Ensure Consistent Maven Versions
items.author: Govinda Solanki
items.content:
When working on a project, it’s important to ensure that everyone on your team is using the same version of Apache Maven. This helps avoid issues that can arise from different Maven versions. One easy way to achieve this is by using the Maven Wrapper.

Maven Wrapper is a tool that allows you to specify a particular Maven version for your project. It ensures that anyone who clones your project can build it with the exact same Maven version, without needing to install Maven manually.

How to Set Up Maven Wrapper

To set up Maven Wrapper for your project, follow these simple steps:

  1. and navigate to your project directory.
  2. Run the following command to create the Maven Wrapper configuration:
mvn wrapper:wrapper -Dmaven="3.9.9"
  1. This command generates the necessary files and a mvnw command that will download and run Maven version 3.9.9.
  2. Commit the generated files to your version control system (e.g., Git). This ensures that everyone who clones your project will have access to the Maven Wrapper.

Once the Maven Wrapper is set up, you can use it to run Maven commands. Instead of using mvn, use ./mvnw (or mvnw.cmd on Windows). For example:

This command will use the specified Maven version (3.9.9 in this case) to clean and build your project.

Using Maven Wrapper offers several benefits for DevOps teams:

  • : Ensures that all team members and CI/CD pipelines use the same Maven version.
  • : New team members can get started quickly without worrying about installing the correct Maven version.
  • : Simplifies the setup of build environments, making it easier to automate builds and deployments.

Did you know that Maven Wrapper can also help you manage different Maven versions for different projects? If you work on multiple projects that require different Maven versions, Maven Wrapper makes it easy to switch between them without any hassle.

items.title: Microservices vs Monolith: A Complete Architecture Guide for Modern Software Development
items.author: Eltac Shixseyidov
items.content:
Microservices vs Monolith: A Complete Architecture Guide for Modern Software Development

In the ever-evolving landscape of software architecture, one of the most debated topics remains the choice between microservices and monolithic architectures. This decision can make or break your application’s scalability, maintainability, and overall success. Whether you’re a startup founder, a seasoned architect, or a developer preparing for your next interview, understanding these architectural patterns is crucial for making informed decisions.

Understanding the Fundamentals

What is a Monolithic Architecture?

A monolithic architecture is a traditional software design pattern where all components of an application are interconnected and deployed as a single unit. Think of it as a large, self-contained building where all rooms are connected through internal corridors — you can’t move one room without affecting the entire structure.

  • Shared database and runtime
  • Internal method calls for communication
  • Centralized business logic

Microservices architecture breaks down an application into a collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service is responsible for a specific business function and communicates with others through well-defined APIs. Imagine a modern shopping mall where each store operates independently but contributes to the overall shopping experience.

  • Multiple small, independent services
  • Service-specific databases
  • Network-based communication (REST, gRPC, messaging)

The Historical Context: From Monoliths to Microservices

The journey from monolithic to microservices architecture didn’t happen overnight. It evolved through several phases:

  1. : Everything ran on massive, centralized systems
  2. : Introduction of distributed computing concepts
  3. Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA): Early attempts at service decomposition
  4. : Netflix, Amazon, and other tech giants pioneered the modern approach

Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA): The Bridge Between Monoliths and Microservices

Before diving deeper into microservices vs monoliths, it’s essential to understand SOA, which serves as the conceptual bridge between these two approaches.

Service-Oriented Architecture is a design paradigm that structures applications as a collection of services that communicate through well-defined interfaces. SOA predates microservices and laid the groundwork for distributed system thinking.

  • Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) for communication
  • Heavyweight protocols (SOAP, WS-*)

SOA vs Microservices: Key Differences

Aspect SOA Microservices Communication ESB-based, heavyweight protocols Lightweight protocols (REST, messaging) Data Management Shared databases Database per service Governance Centralized Decentralized Service Size Larger, more comprehensive Smaller, focused on single responsibility Technology Stack Standardized across enterprise Technology diversity allowed

Other Architecture Types Worth Knowing

A hybrid approach that maintains the single deployment unit of a monolith while organizing code into distinct, loosely coupled modules.

  • Medium-sized applications
  • Teams that want monolith benefits with better organization
  • Gradual migration path to microservices

2. Serverless Architecture

Event-driven architecture where code runs in stateless compute containers managed by cloud providers.

  • Function as a Service (FaaS)

3. Event-Driven Architecture

Architecture pattern where services communicate through events, promoting loose coupling and asynchronous processing.

  • Real-time processing capabilities

Real-World Scenarios and Use Cases

When to Choose Monolithic Architecture

Scenario 1: Early-Stage Startup A fintech startup building their first MVP with a small team of 3–5 developers.

  • Faster initial development
  • Simpler deployment and debugging
  • Lower operational overhead
  • Easier to iterate and pivot

Scenario 2: Simple CRUD Application A content management system for a local newspaper.

  • Straightforward business logic
  • Limited scalability requirements
  • Cost-effective maintenance

When to Choose Microservices Architecture

Scenario 1: E-commerce Platform (Amazon-scale) A large e-commerce platform serving millions of users globally.

  • Different scaling requirements for catalog, payment, inventory
  • Multiple teams working independently
  • Need for technology diversity
  • High availability requirements

Scenario 2: Streaming Service (Netflix-like) A video streaming platform with complex recommendation algorithms.

  • User management, content delivery, recommendations are distinct domains
  • Different performance characteristics
  • Need for rapid feature deployment
  • Global scale requirements

Detailed Comparison: Monoliths vs Microservices

Development and Deployment

  • : Single codebase, shared development environment
  • : Easier integration testing, but longer test cycles
  • : Single deployment unit, all-or-nothing updates
  • : Simple but affects entire application

Microservices Architecture:

  • : Multiple codebases, independent development cycles
  • : Complex integration testing, faster unit testing
  • : Independent service deployment, gradual rollouts possible
  • : Granular rollbacks, but dependency management complexity

Performance and Scalability

  • Lower latency for internal communications
  • Shared resources can lead to performance bottlenecks
  • Vertical scaling limitations
  • Resource utilization can be inefficient

Microservices Performance:

  • Network latency between services
  • Independent scaling of bottleneck services
  • Better resource utilization
  • Horizontal scaling advantages

Monolithic Data Approach:

  • Single database, ACID transactions
  • Potential for database bottlenecks

Microservices Data Approach:

  • Database per service principle
  • Eventual consistency challenges
  • Data synchronization complexity
  • Better data isolation and security

Team Structure and Conway’s Law

Conway’s Law states: “Organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structure.”

Monolithic Team Structure:

  • Single team or closely coordinated teams
  • Shared responsibility for entire codebase
  • Centralized decision making

Microservices Team Structure:

  • Cross-functional teams owning specific services
  • Independent decision making within service boundaries
  • Clear ownership and accountability
  • Requires mature DevOps practices

Common Interview Questions and Answers

Question 1: “When would you choose a monolith over microservices?”

“I would choose a monolith when:

  • The team size is small (under 10 developers)
  • The application domain is well-understood and unlikely to change dramatically
  • Time-to-market is critical for an MVP
  • The operational complexity of distributed systems isn’t justified by the business requirements
  • The organization lacks mature DevOps practices

For example, a startup building their first product would benefit from a monolith’s simplicity, allowing them to iterate quickly and validate their business model before considering the complexity of microservices.”

Question 2: “How do you handle data consistency in microservices?”

“Data consistency in microservices requires a shift from ACID transactions to eventual consistency patterns:

  1. : Coordinate transactions across services using choreography or orchestration
  2. : Store events that represent state changes, allowing reconstruction of current state
  3. : Separate read and write models for better scalability and consistency
  4. : Implement rollback logic for failed distributed transactions

The key is to design services with proper boundaries so that most operations can be completed within a single service, minimizing the need for distributed transactions.”

Question 3: “What are the main challenges of microservices architecture?”

“The main challenges include:

  1. Distributed System Complexity: Network failures, latency, debugging across services
  2. : Eventual consistency, distributed transactions, data synchronization
  3. : API versioning, service discovery, circuit breakers
  4. Monitoring and Observability: Distributed tracing, log aggregation, metrics collection
  5. : Container orchestration, CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure management
  6. : Integration testing, contract testing, end-to-end testing complexity
  7. : Service-to-service authentication, network security, data protection”

Question 4: “How do you decompose a monolith into microservices?”

“I follow a strategic approach:

  1. : Identify bounded contexts and business capabilities
  2. : Gradually replace monolith components with microservices
  3. : Start with read replicas, then separate write databases
  4. : Introduce to manage service routing and cross-cutting concerns
  5. Extract Services Gradually: Begin with least dependent, most business-critical services
  6. : Plan for data consistency during transition
  7. : Implement comprehensive testing at each step

The key is to do this incrementally, ensuring system stability throughout the migration.”

Question 5: “How do microservices handle communication failures?”

“Microservices must be designed for failure:

  1. : Prevent cascading failures by stopping calls to unhealthy services
  2. Retry with Exponential Backoff: Handle transient failures gracefully
  3. : Set appropriate timeouts to prevent hanging operations
  4. : Isolate resources to prevent total system failure
  5. : Provide degraded functionality when dependencies fail
  6. : Monitor service health and remove unhealthy instances
  7. Asynchronous Communication: Use messaging for non-critical operations to reduce coupling”

Migration Strategies: Moving Between Architectures

Monolith to Microservices Migration

The Strangler Fig Pattern:

  1. Identify service boundaries using domain-driven design
  2. Create new microservice alongside monolith
  3. Route new features to microservice
  4. Gradually migrate existing functionality
  5. Remove code from monolith once migration is complete

Database Decomposition Strategy:

  1. Start with shared database
  2. Create service-specific read replicas
  3. Implement data synchronization mechanisms
  4. Finally separate databases completely

Microservices to Monolith Migration

Sometimes, teams realize they’ve over-engineered their solution. The reverse migration involves:

  1. Identifying service boundaries that don’t align with business needs
  2. Consolidating related services
  3. Merging databases where appropriate
  4. Simplifying deployment and monitoring

Technology Stack Considerations

Monolithic Technology Choices

: Java, C#, Python, Ruby, Node.js : Spring Boot, ASP.NET Core, Django, Rails, Express : PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server : Traditional servers, containers, cloud platforms

Microservices Technology Ecosystem

: Kong, Ambassador, AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management : Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect : Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, AWS ECS : Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS, Azure Service Bus : Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, Zipkin, ELK Stack : Consul, Eureka, Kubernetes DNS

Performance and Monitoring Considerations

  • Application Performance Monitoring (APM) tools
  • Database performance monitoring
  • Server resource monitoring
  • Single point of logging and metrics

: Track requests across multiple services Service Mesh Observability: Monitor service-to-service communication : Aggregate logs from all services : Monitor individual service health : Define and monitor service level objectives

  • Single authentication point
  • Centralized authorization
  • Service-to-service authentication (mTLS)
  • Distributed authorization
  • Secrets management across services

Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

  • Lower development complexity
  • Simpler infrastructure requirements
  • Reduced operational overhead
  • Fewer specialized skills needed
  • Higher infrastructure costs (container orchestration, service mesh)
  • Increased operational complexity
  • Need for specialized DevOps skills
  • More sophisticated monitoring and tooling

When the Investment Pays Off

Microservices typically provide ROI when:

  • Application serves millions of users
  • Multiple teams need to work independently
  • Different parts of the system have vastly different scaling requirements
  • Technology diversity provides competitive advantages

My Considerations and Recommendations

After working with both architectures across various projects and scales, here are my practical recommendations:

Start with a Modular Monolith

For most projects, I recommend starting with a well-structured modular monolith. This approach provides:

  • Clear service boundaries from day one
  • Ability to migrate to microservices when needed
  • Lower complexity during initial development
  • Better understanding of domain boundaries before committing to distributed architecture

The “Microservices Readiness” Checklist

Before moving to microservices, ensure your organization has:

  • [ ] Mature CI/CD practices
  • [ ] Container orchestration expertise
  • [ ] Distributed systems monitoring capabilities
  • [ ] Multiple teams that would benefit from independent deployment
  • [ ] Clear understanding of service boundaries
  • [ ] DevOps culture and practices

The Sweet Spot: Hybrid Approaches

In practice, the most successful architectures often combine elements of both:

  • Core business logic in microservices
  • Supporting functionality in modular components
  • Shared infrastructure services
  • Event-driven communication where appropriate

Technology-First vs Domain-First Thinking

Avoid the trap of choosing architecture based solely on technology trends. Instead:

  1. Start with business requirements
  2. Consider team structure and capabilities
  3. Evaluate scalability needs
  4. Assess operational maturity
  5. Then choose the appropriate architecture

Think of architecture as evolutionary, not revolutionary:

  • Phase 1: Well-structured monolith with clear module boundaries
  • Phase 2: Extract high-value, independent services
  • Phase 3: Gradually decompose based on business needs and scaling requirements
  • Phase 4: Mature microservices ecosystem with full observability

The choice between microservices and monolithic architecture isn’t binary — it’s a spectrum of trade-offs that depend on your specific context, team capabilities, and business requirements. While microservices offer compelling benefits for large-scale, complex systems, monoliths remain an excellent choice for many applications.

The key to success lies not in following architectural trends, but in understanding your requirements, constraints, and team capabilities. Start simple, build incrementally, and evolve your architecture as your understanding of the domain and scaling requirements becomes clearer.

Remember: the best architecture is the one that enables your team to deliver value to customers efficiently while maintaining system reliability and developer productivity. Sometimes that’s a well-crafted monolith, sometimes it’s a sophisticated microservices ecosystem, and often it’s something in between.

Whether you’re building the next unicorn startup or modernizing enterprise systems, the principles outlined in this guide will help you make informed architectural decisions that serve your business goals both today and in the future.

items.title: Avoiding Routing Hell: Traefik, Docker Networks, and the Multi-App Deployment Trap
items.author: Ananda Fikri Ijlal Akbar
items.content:
This has been a fun and insightful journey — a hands-on exploration of deploying multiple apps on a single server.

So, here’s the context: I wanted to deploy two containerized applications on my single server:

  1. — for Capture The Flag competitions
  2. — for monitoring and visualization

Each of them was already mapped to its own subdomain:

  1. https://ctf.<mydomain>.com
  2. https://grafana.<mydomain>.com
Fun fact: both applications run on the same server, and I used Docker to manage them.

Now comes the big question:

How can I make both applications accessible over and without any conflicts?

That’s exactly what I experimented with, and the answer lies in using a reverse proxy.

This article is more of a and a reflection of my practical experience. You probably won’t be able to fully replicate it using this article — but I’ve got your back.

Have you ever heard about ?

Traefik is a modern reverse proxy and load balancer made specifically for microservices and containerized apps. It integrates super well with Docker, supports automatic SSL with Let’s Encrypt, and handles routing based on domain names or labels. Pretty sweet, right?

In this experiment, I used to route incoming traffic to the correct Docker container based on the domain name. This allowed me to bind web traffic to a single entry point (ports 80 and 443), without port clashes between services.

First, here’s the problem. I had already configured Traefik with the following traefik.yml as my default setup.

entryPoints:  web:  websecure:  docker:    watch: true  myresolver:      email: <myemail>@gmail.com      httpChallenge:  level: DEBUG

The config above sets the default route through ports and with automatic SSL registration. I also set up Traefik in traefik-docker-compose.yml to act as the default middleware between the application containers and public access.

services:  traefik:    container_name: traefik    ports:      - "443:443"      - ./traefik.yml:/traefik.yml:ro      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro      - --configFile=/traefik.yml      - --accesslog=true      - traefik_proxy  traefik_proxy:

In that configuration, you’ll see a networks section defining traefik_proxy as a Docker network group.

I deployed it using the following command:

docker compose -f traefik-docker-compose.yml up -d --build

But this is still a basic configuration.

In Traefik, we also need to specify container labels, which are used to route each specific web application through Traefik.

labels:      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.<unique_labels>.rule=Host(`<subdomain>.<mydomain>.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.<unique_labels>.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.<unique_labels>.tls.certresolver=myresolver"

Next, I created two applications running in Docker. One of them is , which I use as the CTF platform. I modified the Docker Compose file to work with Traefik, and saved it as ctfd-docker-compose.yml, with the following content:

services:  ctfd:    user: root    ports:    environment:
      - UPLOAD_FOLDER=/var/uploads
      - DATABASE_URL=mysql+pymysql://ctfd:ctfd@db/ctfd
      - REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379
      - WORKERS=1
      - LOG_FOLDER=/var/log/CTFd
      - ACCESS_LOG=-      - REVERSE_PROXY=true      - .data/CTFd/logs:/var/log/CTFd
      - .data/CTFd/uploads:/var/uploads
      - .:/opt/CTFd:ro      - db        default:    image: nginx:stable    volumes:
      - ./conf/nginx/http.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
    ports:    depends_on:    labels:      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.rule=Host(`ctf.<mydomain>.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.tls.certresolver=myresolver"        default:    image: mariadb:10.11    environment:
      - MARIADB_ROOT_PASSWORD=xxx
      - MARIADB_USER=xxx      - MARIADB_DATABASE=xxx    volumes:
      - .data/mysql:/var/lib/mysql
    networks:    # This command is required to set important mariadb defaults
    command: [mysqld, --character-set-server=utf8mb4, --collation-server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci, --wait_timeout=28800, --log-warnings=0]
    image: redis:4    volumes:    networks:    default:        internal: true        external: true

Next, I also set up the Grafana Docker Compose configuration — grafana-docker-compose.yml — as follows:

version: '3'  grafana:      context: ./compose/grafana/    restart: always      - 3000      - GF_AUTH_ANONYMOUS_ENABLED=false
      - GF_AUTH_BASIC_ENABLED=false
      - GF_ENABLE_GZIP=true      - grafana-data:/var/lib/grafana      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.rule=Host(`grafana.<mydomain>.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.tls.certresolver=myresolver"
    networks:        traefik_proxy:    build:    container_name: loki      - 3100:3100    command: -config.file=/etc/loki/local-config.yaml      - promtail    build:
      context: ./compose/promtail/
    container_name: promtail      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
    command: -config.file=/etc/promtail/config.yaml
#     build:
#       context: ./compose/prometheus/
#     container_name: prometheus#       - 9090:9090#     links:#       - blackbox#     build: 
#       context: ./compose/node/
#     container_name: node#       - 9100:9100#     volumes:
#       - /proc:/host/proc:ro
#       - /sys:/host/sys:ro#     command:
#       - '--path.procfs=/host/proc'
#       - '--path.rootfs=/rootfs'
#       - '--path.sysfs=/host/sys'
#       - '--collector.filesystem.mount-points-exclude=^/(sys|proc|dev|host|etc)($$|/)'#     build: 
#       context: ./compose/blackbox/
#     container_name: blackbox#       - 9115:9115  grafana-data:  traefik_proxy:

I run those with following commands to provision the page:

docker compose -f ctfd-docker-compose.yml up -d --builddocker compose -f grafana-docker-compose.yml up -d --build

And I check log, it show the following:

2025-05-25T04:13:45Z DBG github.com/traefik/traefik/v3/pkg/server/configurationwatcher.go:227 > Configuration received config={"http":{"routers":{"grafana":{"entryPoints":["websecure"],"rule":"Host(`grafana.<yourdomain>.com`)","service":"grafana","tls":{"certResolver":"myresolver"}},"nginxctf":{"entryPoints":["websecure"],"rule":"Host(`ctf.<yourdomain>.com`)","service":"nginxctf-traefik","tls":{"certResolver":"myresolver"}}},"services":{"grafana":{"loadBalancer":{"passHostHeader":true,"responseForwarding":{"flushInterval":"100ms"},"servers":[{"url":"http://172.23.0.3:3000"}],"strategy":"wrr"}},"nginxctf-traefik":{"loadBalancer":{"passHostHeader":true,"responseForwarding":{"flushInterval":"100ms"},"servers":[{"url":"http://172.22.0.3:80"}],"strategy":"wrr"}}}},"tcp":{},"tls":{},"udp":{}} providerName=docker

It shows all services are running, but if we try to access, Grafana Application can run smoothly.

But if we try to access https://ctf.<mydomain>.com, it shows a  error.

I tried curling and checking communication between the CTFd, Nginx, and Traefik containers — they can all talk to each other on the traefik_proxy network. But why can’t Traefik expose the CTFd endpoint?

This behavior is kinda strange, right?

But logically, I found that Traefik follows this flow to manage proxying and redirect endpoints on the same port.

Based on the image above, I noticed that the Traefik handler gets confused because, within the same group, there are two middleware labels defined for Traefik to manage.

So Traefik prioritizes in the early network and ignores or skips the other in the same Docker network group.

That’s why I can access the application, but not the application.

In this experimental approach, I solved Traefik’s routing confusion by creating separate Docker network groups specifically for Traefik connections:

    The idea behind separating these network groups is to help clearly recognize each middleware managing its respective service labels, avoiding confusion from round-robin conflicts.

    docker network create ctf_traefik_proxydocker network create grafana_traefik_proxy

    This way, won’t ignore any application services running on the same server.

    Another creative way to make it work is by creating a dedicated Docker Compose file called application-service.yml. In this file, I only define the applications that need routing through Traefik , purely for management purposes.

    services:  nginxctf:    container_name: nginxctf    volumes:
          - <HOME_PATH>/ctfd/conf/nginx/http.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
        networks:    labels:      - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.rule=Host(`ctf.<mydomain>.com`)"
          - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.entrypoints=websecure"
          - "traefik.http.routers.nginxctf.tls.certresolver=myresolver"    build:
          context: <HOME_PATH>/grafana-lab/compose/grafana/
        container_name: grafana    environment:
          - GF_AUTH_ANONYMOUS_ENABLED=false
          - GF_AUTH_BASIC_ENABLED=false    volumes:
          - grafana-data:/var/lib/grafana
        networks:    labels:      - "traefik.http.routers.grafana.rule=Host(`grafana.<mydomain>.com`)"
          - "traefik.http.routers.grafana.entrypoints=websecure"
          - "traefik.http.routers.grafana.tls.certresolver=myresolver"
          - "traefik.http.services.grafana.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
      grafana-data:  ctf_traefik_proxy:  grafana_traefik_proxy:

    I also defined in ctfd-docker-compose.yml to connect with the ctf_traefik_proxy network, so the nginxctf container can communicate with the CTFd container to load content properly.

    services:  ctfd:    user: root    ports:    environment:
          - UPLOAD_FOLDER=/var/uploads
          - DATABASE_URL=mysql+pymysql://ctfd:ctfd@db/ctfd
          - REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379
          - WORKERS=1
          - LOG_FOLDER=/var/log/CTFd
          - ACCESS_LOG=-      - REVERSE_PROXY=true      - .data/CTFd/logs:/var/log/CTFd
          - .data/CTFd/uploads:/var/uploads
          - .:/opt/CTFd:ro      - db        internal:    image: mariadb:10.11    environment:
          - MARIADB_ROOT_PASSWORD=xxx
          - MARIADB_USER=xxx      - MARIADB_DATABASE=xxx    volumes:
          - .data/mysql:/var/lib/mysql
        networks:    # This command is required to set important mariadb defaults
        command: [mysqld, --character-set-server=utf8mb4, --collation-server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci, --wait_timeout=28800, --log-warnings=0]
        image: redis:4    volumes:    networks:    internal:    ctf_traefik_proxy:

    I also updated the traefik-docker-compose.yml file to register the containers with the ctf_traefik_proxy and grafana_traefik_proxy network groups.

    services:  traefik:    container_name: traefik    ports:      - "443:443"      - ./traefik.yml:/traefik.yml:ro      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro      - --configFile=/traefik.yml      - --accesslog=true      - ctf_traefik_proxy  ctf_traefik_proxy:  grafana_traefik_proxy:

    Next, I ran all the updated compose files along with the application-service.yml file using the following command:

    Next, I ran all the updated compose files, including application-service.yml, with the following commands:

    docker compose -f <traefik_directory>/traefik-docker-compose up -d --builddocker compose -f <ctfd_directory>/ctfd-docker-compose.yml up -d --build
    docker compose -f <grafana_application_directory>/grafana-docker-compose.yml up -d --build
    docker compose -f application-service.yml up -d --build

    After that, I checked every Docker network group to make sure the communication was properly established according to the connected containers.

    docker network inspect ctf_traefik_proxy
    ......[SNIP]......        "Containers": {
                "285d69576d6566dc61accad4ea15026a289c61f3802f2ce649c0fe45ebf20640": {
                    "Name": "ctfd",                "MacAddress": "aaaaaa",
                    "IPv4Address": "172.25.0.3/16",
                    "IPv6Address": ""            "3584e03172e56da6df834599911e7dab1959299841d5b9bf16887a40e3d199e4": {                "EndpointID": "xxx",                "IPv4Address": "172.25.0.2/16",            },
                "812b2458d837a713e55d2fc3b0b1aa56544a31938e2de6990bc64d1a8cd7b3ab": {
                    "Name": "nginxctf",                "MacAddress": "yyyyyy",
                    "IPv4Address": "172.25.0.4/16",
                    "IPv6Address": ""        },        "Labels": {}
    docker network inspect grafana_traefik_proxy
    ......[SNIP]......        "Containers": {
                "3584e03172e56da6df834599911e7dab1959299841d5b9bf16887a40e3d199e4": {
                    "Name": "traefik",                "MacAddress": "xxxxxx",
                    "IPv4Address": "172.24.0.2/16",
                    "IPv6Address": ""            "f67c557802ddf056ae32103b5b0031797739c453376306ec95766dad0c746ee4": {                "EndpointID": "yyy",                "IPv4Address": "172.24.0.3/16",            }        "Options": {},    }

    If you look closely, the network groups specified with don’t conflict since they’re in separate Docker network groups.

    Next, when we visit each site, everything runs smoothly. Here’s the result after successfully routed my other application.

    Based on the results of running multiple with on a single server, my two are working as expected.

    Running multiple containerized applications on a single server with Traefik is totally doable as long as you manage your Docker networks and Traefik middleware labels properly.

    Separating Docker network groups per app clears up Traefik’s routing confusion and avoids gateway timeouts.

    This approach keeps your infrastructure clean, scalable, and ready for more apps without port conflicts.

    So yeah, Traefik + Docker network Group Management = couple for single-server multi-app setups. Simple, smart, and slick.
